description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how multi-head attention parallelizes attention across subspaces, helping students understand why splitting into heads improves representational capacity over single attention.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, especially to contrast single-head vs multi-head attention and explain the concatenation + projection step.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.
teaching_value: Provides a complete structural overview of the Transformer, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact, where each component sits, and how information flows from input to output.
when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson or when transitioning from individual components to the full model, as a reference map students can return to.
suggested_caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions, with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white).
teaching_value: Makes the abstract sinusoidal encoding formula concrete by showing the actual pattern it produces, helping students understand how position information is encoded as unique frequency patterns across dimensions.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the positional encoding formula to give students an intuition for what the encoding looks like and why different positions produce distinguishable patterns.
suggested_caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.

description: Visual illustration of Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position index.
teaching_value: Clarifies the geometric intuition behind RoPE — that relative position is encoded as a rotation — helping students understand why it generalizes better than additive positional encodings.
when_to_show: Show when teaching relative positional encodings, especially after sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs rotational approaches and explain RoPE's advantages for long-context tasks.
suggested_caption: RoPE encodes position as rotation in 2D subspaces of the embedding. (Su et al., 2021)

description: Side-by-side comparison of training phases for vanilla Transformer (left) and Transformer-XL (right) with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.
teaching_value: Directly illustrates the key innovation of Transformer-XL — segment-level recurrence — by contrasting it with the fixed-context vanilla Transformer, making the concept of extended attention span tangible.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and how Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism solves it.
suggested_caption: Transformer-XL caches prior segment states to extend attention span. (Dai et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory types: regular memory (recent past activations) and compressed memory (older activations compressed by a compression function), with attention attending to both.
teaching_value: Illustrates the two-tier memory hierarchy of the Compressive Transformer, helping students understand how compression extends temporal range beyond Transformer-XL while managing memory cost.
when_to_show: Show after Transformer-XL to demonstrate how compressive memory further extends context, and when discussing tradeoffs between memory size and temporal range.
suggested_caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right) in the Compressive Transformer, demonstrating that compressed memory receives meaningful attention despite being older.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that compressed memory is actually attended to, validating the design choice and helping students understand that older compressed representations retain useful information.
when_to_show: Show when discussing whether compression loses important information, as empirical evidence that the model attends to compressed memory and it is not wasted.
suggested_caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)

description: Diagram of SPALM architecture showing how short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL-style cache) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce the final output.
teaching_value: Illustrates the distinction between short-term recurrent memory and long-term external memory, and how a gating mechanism integrates both, helping students understand hybrid memory architectures.
when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, especially to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and explain how retrieval-augmented memory works.
suggested_caption: SPALM combines recurrent short-term and k-NN long-term memory via gating. (Yogatama et al., 2021)

description: Line chart comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical efficiency of fine-tuning with k-NN memory augmentation versus training from scratch, helping students understand the cost-benefit tradeoff of adding external memory to pretrained models.
when_to_show: Show when discussing practical deployment of memory-augmented Transformers, particularly to motivate fine-tuning as a cheaper alternative to full retraining.
suggested_caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches scratch-trained Memorizing Transformer performance. (Wu et al., 2022)

description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) showing how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.
teaching_value: Makes the ALiBi mechanism concrete by showing exactly where and how the distance-based bias is applied to attention scores, helping students understand how it encodes relative position without learned embeddings.
when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, especially to contrast with RoPE and learned positional encodings, and when discussing context length extrapolation strategies.
suggested_caption: ALiBi adds a negative linear distance bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)

description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary/RoPE, T5 relative, and ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve, indicating best extrapolation.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates why positional encoding choice matters for length generalization — students can directly compare how each method degrades (or doesn't) when inference context exceeds training context, making the abstract concept of 'extrapolation' concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; especially useful when a student asks 'why does positional encoding matter at inference time?' or when discussing out-of-distribution generalization.
suggested_caption: ALiBi extrapolates best beyond training context length. (Press et al., 2021)

description: Animated-style diagram showing how Universal Transformer processes a sequence by repeatedly applying the same transformation to all positions in parallel across multiple recurrent steps, with arrows indicating the iterative refinement of hidden states h^t.
teaching_value: Clarifies the key innovation of Universal Transformer — that depth is recurrent and shared rather than fixed and distinct — helping students understand how it bridges Transformers and RNNs and why adaptive computation time is needed.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer, particularly when explaining the difference between fixed-depth Transformers and recurrent-depth models, or when discussing parameter sharing across layers.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al., 2019)

description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.
teaching_value: Shows the architectural symmetry between encoder and decoder in Universal Transformer and highlights the cross-attention connection, helping students understand how the recurrent abstraction maps onto a seq2seq framework.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the recurrent step concept (Image 2), when students need to see how Universal Transformer fits into a full seq2seq pipeline or when comparing it to standard Transformer encoder-decoder.
suggested_caption: Universal Transformer encoder and decoder share recurrent structure; decoder cross-attends to h^T. (Dehghani et al., 2019)

description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in Adaptive Attention Span, showing a piecewise linear sigmoid-like curve that maps distance between query and key to a value in [0,1], with the transition region controlled by learnable parameter z.
teaching_value: Makes the mathematical definition of the soft mask tangible — students can see how the function smoothly gates attention weights to zero beyond a learned span, understanding why it is differentiable and thus trainable end-to-end.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how adaptive attention span works mechanically, especially when students struggle to connect the formula m_z to its effect on attention weights or when discussing efficient attention mechanisms.
suggested_caption: Soft mask function smoothly limits attention span via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al., 2019)

description: Diagram illustrating three types of depth-adaptive classifiers in Depth-Adaptive Transformer: (1) sequence-specific exit where all tokens share one exit, (2) token-specific exit where each token has its own exit, and (3) a third variant, each shown with exit classifiers attached at multiple layers.
teaching_value: Concretely distinguishes the three granularities of early-exit strategies, helping students understand the trade-off between computational savings and flexibility, and how the same multi-exit architecture can be used in different ways.
when_to_show: Show when introducing adaptive depth or early-exit models, or when comparing Depth-Adaptive Transformer to CALM; useful when students ask how the model decides how many layers to use.
suggested_caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and mixed. (Elabyad et al., 2020)

description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image pixels in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline), illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood in both flattened and 2D grid layouts.
teaching_value: Visually explains how local attention is applied to image data, making it clear why 2D attention is more natural for images than 1D raster-scan attention, and how the memory matrix M is defined spatially.
when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of vision tasks; useful when students ask how Transformer attention is adapted for 2D spatial data.
suggested_caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for image pixels in Image Transformer. (Parmar et al., 2018)

description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity patterns and corresponding attention matrices for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which; bottom row shows the sparsity pattern in the attention matrix.
teaching_value: Directly contrasts dense vs. sparse attention patterns, making the sparsity structure of strided and fixed attention visually obvious and helping students understand how these patterns reduce the O(n^2) cost while maintaining long-range connectivity.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Sparse Transformer or any sparse attention mechanism; ideal when students need to understand how factorized attention patterns approximate full attention at lower cost.
suggested_caption: Full, strided, and fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al., 2019)

description: Side-by-side visualization of attention patterns for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird models, each showing a combination of local (sliding window), global (special tokens), and in Big Bird's case, random attention connections overlaid on a sequence.
teaching_value: Enables direct visual comparison of three influential efficient Transformer architectures, helping students identify the shared design principle (global + local attention) and the unique addition of random attention in Big Bird.
when_to_show: Show when comparing global-local attention models, or when students ask how Longformer and Big Bird differ from each other and from ETC; useful as a summary diagram after covering each model individually.
suggested_caption: Attention patterns of ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird: local, global, and random.

description: Heatmap-style visualization of the LSH attention matrix showing how queries and keys are grouped into buckets (indicated by colored blocks along the diagonal), with attention restricted to within-bucket pairs, resulting in a block-sparse attention matrix.
teaching_value: Shows the resulting sparsity structure of LSH attention in matrix form, helping students understand that hashing creates approximate nearest-neighbor clusters and why this reduces attention complexity from O(n^2) to near O(n log n).
when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (complement to Image 10), when students need to see the resulting attention matrix structure or when discussing the computational savings of Reformer.
suggested_caption: LSH attention matrix: queries attend only within same hash bucket. (Kitaev et al., 2020)

description: Step-by-step process diagram of LSH attention showing four sequential stages: (1) bucketing queries and keys via random projections, (2) sorting by bucket assignment, (3) chunking into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention within chunks.
teaching_value: Breaks down the LSH attention algorithm into discrete, understandable steps, allowing students to trace how a high-dimensional query is mapped to a bucket and why chunking is needed to handle variable bucket sizes efficiently.
when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; use before Image 9 to build up from the process to the resulting matrix structure, or when students ask how LSH is implemented in practice.
suggested_caption: LSH attention: four steps — bucketing, sorting, chunking, attention. (Kitaev et al., 2020)

description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Linformer architecture diagram showing linear projection layers E_i and F_i added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k. (Right) Log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer vs. standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates how low-rank projection reduces the quadratic attention bottleneck to linear complexity, and the right panel provides empirical evidence of the computational speedup at long sequence lengths.
when_to_show: Show when introducing efficient attention mechanisms, specifically when explaining how low-rank approximations reduce O(L²) attention to O(Lk) and why this matters for long sequences.
suggested_caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel computation order diagram: (Left) Standard softmax attention showing the expensive O(L²) matrix multiplication order (Q·K^T first, then softmax, then multiply V). (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) showing the reordered computation (φ(K)^T·V first) that enables linear-time attention via kernel approximation.
teaching_value: Illustrates the key mathematical insight of RFA: by reordering matrix multiplications using the associative property, the expensive L×L attention matrix is never explicitly computed, reducing complexity from quadratic to linear.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel-based methods approximate softmax attention, particularly to illustrate why reordering operations (right-to-left vs left-to-right) changes computational complexity.
suggested_caption: RFA reorders matrix ops to avoid the L×L attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)

description: Two-panel approximation error comparison for Performer: (Left) Bar/line chart comparing approximation error of i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features across different settings. (Right) Chart comparing approximation error of sin/cos random features vs. positive random features, showing positive features yield lower error.
teaching_value: Provides empirical justification for two key design choices in Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism: using orthogonal rather than i.i.d. features reduces variance, and using positive (non-negative) random features avoids instability from cancellation in softmax approximation.
when_to_show: Show when discussing Performer's FAVOR+ algorithm, specifically to justify why orthogonal and positive random features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos features for approximating softmax attention.
suggested_caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)

description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) Standard Transformer-XL with layer norm in original position, (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm), and (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using reordered layer norm, enabling stable RL training.
teaching_value: Clearly shows the two architectural modifications (layer norm reordering and gating units) that stabilize Transformer training in RL settings, making it easy to compare the incremental changes from baseline to GTrXL.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Transformers applied to reinforcement learning, specifically to explain why standard Transformer-XL is unstable in RL and how gating and layer norm reordering address this.
suggested_caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
